text.title: token-organization Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the token-organization methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Tokens MUST be organised in three tiers: primitives (raw values), semantic (role-based aliases), component (component-scoped). Two-tier or four-tier hierarchies are forbidden.

rationale.source: Nathan Curtis — Naming Tokens in Design Systems

rule.rationale: Two tiers leak primitives into components; four tiers introduce indirection without payoff.

rule.statement: Component-layer tokens MUST NOT reference primitives directly. Component → semantic → primitive is the only allowed reference chain.
rule.rationale: Direct primitive references defeat the semantic abstraction and break on theme switch.

rule.statement: Token names MUST follow `{category}.{property}.{variant}.{state}` with dot separators (e.g., `color.action.primary.hover`). Hyphens or camelCase are forbidden in source.

rationale.source: Adobe Spectrum tokens; Style Dictionary docs

rule.rationale: Dot-separated names parse mechanically into the hierarchy; hyphens lose the structure.

rule.statement: Semantic and component layers MUST NOT contain raw hex / rgb / px values — only references to primitives or other semantic tokens.
rule.rationale: Raw values in upper layers break theming and add maintenance cost.

rule.statement: Interactive tokens MUST carry an explicit state suffix (`.default`, `.hover`, `.active`, `.disabled`, `.focus`). Missing state suffix is forbidden for interactive tokens.
rule.rationale: Missing states force ad-hoc derivation in component code, defeating tokenisation.
